truth.papers.A59.title: Enabling superior impact toughness of low-density δ-ferrite steel by dispersing ultra-fine spheroidized carbides in the ferrite/carbide composite lamellae
truth.papers.A59.paper_scope: The mechanism of the synergistic effect of microstructural evolution on fracture toughness in low-density δ-ferrite steel under varying rolling temperatures was systematically investigated.

prediction.papers.A59.title: Enabling superior impact toughness of low-density δ-ferrite steel by dispersing ultra-fine spheroidized carbides in the ferrite/carbide composite lamellae
prediction.papers.A59.paper_scope: Investigate the synergistic effect of rolling temperature on microstructural evolution, work hardening behavior, and impact toughness in low-density δ-ferrite steel, establishing the correlation between ultra-fine spherical carbides in ferrite/carbide composite lamellae and crack propagation mechanisms.

prediction.alloys.alloy_035c.alloys_notes: Fabricated via vacuum induction melting (VIM). High Al content (~4.5 wt%) expands the δ-ferrite phase region, stabilizing coarse δ-ferrite at room temperature.

truth.processes.proc_035c_650.description: Rolling at 650 °C after homogenization at 1200 °C for 4 h and hot forging to 40 mm, with 60% thickness reduction, followed by air cooling.
truth.processes.proc_035c_750.description: Rolling at 750 °C after homogenization at 1200 °C for 4 h and hot forging to 40 mm, with 60% thickness reduction, followed by air cooling.
truth.processes.proc_035c_850.description: Rolling at 850 °C after homogenization at 1200 °C for 4 h and hot forging to 40 mm, with 60% thickness reduction, followed by air cooling.
truth.processes.proc_035c_950.description: Rolling at 950 °C after homogenization at 1200 °C for 4 h and hot forging to 40 mm, with 60% thickness reduction, followed by air cooling.

prediction.processes.proc_035c_650.description: Homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm and air cooled; reheated to 650°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction from 40 mm to 16 mm, air cooled.
prediction.processes.proc_035c_650.processes_notes: Low-temperature rolling within carbide precipitation stability range (<750°C), enabling ultra-fine spherical carbide formation.
prediction.processes.proc_035c_750.description: Homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm and air cooled; reheated to 750°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction from 40 mm to 16 mm, air cooled.
prediction.processes.proc_035c_750.processes_notes: Low-temperature rolling near upper limit of carbide stability, resulting in coarsened carbides via Ostwald ripening.
prediction.processes.proc_035c_850.description: Homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm and air cooled; reheated to 850°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction from 40 mm to 16 mm, air cooled.
prediction.processes.proc_035c_850.processes_notes: High-temperature rolling within DRX and DIFT range (≥850°C); carbides fully dissolve, forming δ-ferrite and honeycomb martensite lamellae with DIF.
prediction.processes.proc_035c_950.description: Homogenized at 1200°C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm and air cooled; reheated to 950°C for 2 h, hot rolled with 60% reduction from 40 mm to 16 mm, air cooled.
prediction.processes.proc_035c_950.processes_notes: High-temperature rolling with increased austenite stability; higher martensite fraction (59.0%) and coarsened DIF grains.

prediction.processing_steps.sample_R650.processing_: After rolling, air cooled to room temperature.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_R750.processing_: After rolling, air cooled to room temperature.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_R850.processing_: After rolling, air cooled to room temperature.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_R950.processing_: After rolling, air cooled to room temperature.

truth.structures.sample_R650.overall_structure: Lamellar structure with alternating δ-ferrite lamellae and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae, with ultra-fine spherical carbides dispersed in ferrite matrix; no martensite present.
truth.structures.sample_R650.microstructure_co: δ-ferrite (48.5 ± 2.5 vol%) and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae (51.5 ± 2.5 vol%); carbide average size 158.3 ± 76.9 nm, distribution density 1.7 ± 0.2 μm^{-2}
truth.structures.sample_R750.overall_structure: Lamellar structure with alternating δ-ferrite lamellae and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae; carbides coarsened compared to R650, with bimodal distribution (coarse at grain boundaries, fine inside grains).
truth.structures.sample_R750.microstructure_co: δ-ferrite (56.7 ± 3.0 vol%) and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae (43.3 ± 3.0 vol%); carbide average size 247.3 ± 86.8 nm, distribution density 0.6 ± 0.1 μm^{-2}
truth.structures.sample_R750.microstructure_li: 0.6 ± 0.1 μm^{-2} density; coarse at grain boundaries + fine inside grains
truth.structures.sample_R850.overall_structure: Duplex lamellar structure consisting of δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae, with equiaxed DIF (deformation-induced ferrite) grains embedded within the martensite regions. No carbides present.
truth.structures.sample_R850.microstructure_co: δ-ferrite (66.8 ± 3.6 vol%), martensite (33.2 ± 3.6 vol%), and DIF grains; HAGBs proportion 60.1%, GOS≤2° 19.4%
truth.structures.sample_R950.overall_structure: Duplex lamellar structure with δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae, containing coarser equiaxed DIF grains. No carbides present.
truth.structures.sample_R950.microstructure_co: δ-ferrite (41.0 ± 4.2 vol%), martensite (59.0 ± 4.2 vol%), and DIF grains; HAGBs proportion 75.7%, GOS≤2° 25.8%

prediction.structures.sample_R650.overall_structure: Lamellar structure with alternating δ-ferrite lamellae (light areas) and carbide/ferrite (C/F) composite lamellae (dark areas), both showing continuous banded distribution parallel to the rolling direction.
prediction.structures.sample_R650.microstructure_li: lamellar, continuous bands parallel to RD
lamellar bands containing ultra-fine spherical carbides dispersed in ferrite matrix
HAGBs proportion 49.6 %, GOS≤2° 12.3 %, average KAM ~0.48–0.55°, dominant orientation <001>
1.7 ± 0.2 μm^-2; ultra-fine spherical, high-density dispersed within ferrite matrix of composite lamellae; mainly concentrated outside δ-ferrite lamellae
prediction.structures.sample_R750.overall_structure: Lamellar structure with alternating δ-ferrite lamellae and carbide/ferrite composite lamellae; carbides exhibit coarsened distribution of coarse particles at grain boundaries and fine particles inside grains.
prediction.structures.sample_R750.microstructure_li: lamellar, continuous bands parallel to RD
lamellar bands with coarsened spherical carbides in ferrite matrix
HAGBs proportion 43.5 %, GOS≤2° 6.9 %, average KAM ~0.48–0.55°, dominant orientation <001>
0.6 ± 0.1 μm^-2; distribution characteristic of coarse carbides at grain boundaries plus fine carbides inside grains due to Ostwald ripening
prediction.structures.sample_R850.overall_structure: Dual-phase lamellar structure consisting of δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae containing equiaxed deformation-induced ferrite (DIF) grains; carbides completely dissolved.
prediction.structures.sample_R850.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains enclosed within honeycomb martensite lamellae
HAGBs proportion 60.1 %, GOS≤2° 19.4 %, average KAM ~0.3–0.4°, dominant orientation shifting to <101>
prediction.structures.sample_R950.overall_structure: Dual-phase lamellar structure consisting of δ-ferrite lamellae and honeycomb martensite lamellae containing equiaxed DIF grains that grew significantly; carbides completely dissolved.
prediction.structures.sample_R950.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains enclosed within honeycomb martensite lamellae, grown significantly compared to R850
HAGBs proportion 75.7 %, GOS≤2° 25.8 %, average KAM ~0.3–0.4°, dominant orientation <101>

truth.interfaces.sample_R650.phases[ferrite].c: no (inferred from hardness mismatch and strain partitioning)
truth.interfaces.sample_R650.phases[carbide].c: likely semi-coherent or incoherent (high interfacial bonding mentioned)
truth.interfaces.sample_R650.defect_interactio: crack deflection and blunting at carbide/ferrite lamellae
Cracks propagating in δ-ferrite lamellae are arrested and blunted at carbide/ferrite interfaces; carbides increase effective crack tip curvature radius, reducing stress concentration.
Spherical carbides act as nucleation sites for microvoids, inducing horizontal crack propagation along lamellar boundaries, dissipating energy.
truth.interfaces.sample_R650.interface_notes[0: Stress concentration at crack tip in δ-ferrite lamellae is relieved by interface separation in carbide/ferrite lamellae.
truth.interfaces.sample_R650.interface_notes[1: Dislocation pile-up at carbide/ferrite interface due to Orowan mechanism; good interfacial incompatibility.
truth.interfaces.sample_R650.phase_evolution: During low-temperature rolling, supersaturated martensite decomposes, C, Cr, Mn enrich at defect sites, precipitating ultra-fine spherical carbides (M3C, M23C6, M7C3) within ferrite matrix; carbides grow by Ostwald ripening with increasing temperature.
truth.interfaces.sample_R750.defect_interactio: crack deflection weakened due to coarser carbides
Coarser carbides provide less blunting effect, leading to reduced crack tip curvature and easier crack propagation.
truth.interfaces.sample_R750.interface_notes[0: Weakened dispersion of stress by coarser carbides.
truth.interfaces.sample_R750.interface_notes[1: Reduced dislocation pinning due to fewer and coarser carbides.
truth.interfaces.sample_R750.phase_evolution: Ostwald ripening at 750 °C causes dissolution of smaller carbides and growth of larger ones, resulting in bimodal distribution; overall carbide volume fraction decreases.
truth.interfaces.sample_R850.phases[martensite: likely semi-coherent (both BCC/BCT with small lattice mismatch)
truth.interfaces.sample_R850.defect_interactio: Large hardness mismatch causes stress concentration at δ-ferrite/martensite and martensite/DIF interfaces; equiaxed DIF grains act as pre-existing defects promoting brittle fracture.
truth.interfaces.sample_R850.interface_notes[0: Interfacial stress concentration leads to crack initiation at boundaries.
truth.interfaces.sample_R850.interface_notes[1: Deformation coordination hampered by hardness mismatch; brittle fracture through cleavage in DIF grains.
truth.interfaces.sample_R850.phase_evolution: At high temperature, carbides dissolve completely; DIFT (deformation-induced ferrite transformation) occurs during rolling, forming equiaxed ferrite grains within austenite, which later transforms to martensite, resulting in honeycomb structure.
truth.interfaces.sample_R950.defect_interactio: Similar to R850 but with larger martensite fraction and coarser DIF grains, further reducing toughness.
truth.interfaces.sample_R950.interface_notes[0: Stress concentration at interfaces promotes brittle crack propagation.
truth.interfaces.sample_R950.interface_notes[1: Limited plastic deformation due to high martensite hardness; cracks propagate through martensite and along DIF interfaces.
truth.interfaces.sample_R950.phase_evolution: With increasing temperature, DIFT and DRX produce more equiaxed ferrite grains and coarser martensite lamellae; all carbides dissolved.

prediction.interfaces.sample_R650.defect_interactio: dislocation pinning by spherical carbides (Orowan mechanism)

Carbides pin dislocations and increase work hardening rate of composite lamellae; part of interfacial stress between ferrite matrix and carbides is transferred to carbides, alleviating stress concentration.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R650.interface_notes[0: During impact, interface separation occurs at lamellar boundaries dispersing crack-tip stress on the separation surface; spherical carbides increase effective curvature radius of crack tip reducing stress concentration factor according to elasticity theory.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R750.defect_interactio: dislocation pinning by spherical carbides (Orowan mechanism)
Weakened Orowan effect due to carbide coarsening and reduced distribution density; coarse particles have weakened ability to disperse stress at crack tip.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R750.interface_notes[0: Coarse carbides at grain boundaries and fine carbides inside grains due to Ostwald ripening; reduced crack deflection resistance and lower number of microvoid nucleation sites compared to R650.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R850.defect_interactio: stress concentration at phase boundaries due to hardness mismatch
Maximum hardness difference reaches 3.86 GPa (martensite 6.0 GPa vs δ-ferrite 2.6 GPa), causing stress concentration at phase boundaries and preferential crack initiation sites.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R850.interface_notes[0: Large hardness mismatch between martensite, DIF, and δ-ferrite induces stress concentration at phase interfaces; equiaxed DIF grains act as pre-existing defects guiding rapid cleavage crack propagation.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R850.phase_evolution: Carbides completely dissolved at high temperature; austenite stabilized by Mn and high temperature; after rolling, residual austenite transformed to martensite, resulting in honeycomb martensite lamellae with embedded equiaxed DIF grains.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R950.defect_interactio: stress concentration at phase boundaries due to hardness mismatch
Maximum hardness difference reaches 3.86 GPa (martensite 6.5 GPa vs δ-ferrite 2.8 GPa), causing stress concentration at phase boundaries and brittle fracture behavior.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R950.interface_notes[0: Martensite/ferrite hardness mismatch >3.3 GPa hinders deformation coordination; stress concentration at phase boundaries combined with DIF grains acting as pre-existing defects leads to unstable crack propagation immediately after initiation.
prediction.interfaces.sample_R950.phase_evolution: Increased martensite fraction (59.0%) and DIF grain coarsening compared to R850; high-temperature rolling significantly degrades toughness through microstructure coarsening and brittle phase formation.

truth.properties.sample_R650.mechanical.tensil: Initial rapid decrease, then hump at strain ~0.02–0.03, finally slow decay
truth.properties.sample_R650.mechanical.impact: Charpy V-notch, room temperature, direction perpendicular to RD
truth.properties.sample_R750.mechanical.tensil: Hump shape similar to R650 but slightly lower values; initial decrease, hump, then slow decay
truth.properties.sample_R750.mechanical.impact: Charpy V-notch, room temperature, direction perpendicular to RD
truth.properties.sample_R850.mechanical.tensil: Monotonically decreasing without hump; work hardening rate drops rapidly
truth.properties.sample_R850.mechanical.impact: Charpy V-notch, room temperature, direction perpendicular to RD
truth.properties.sample_R950.mechanical.tensil: Monotonically decreasing without hump; very low work hardening capacity
truth.properties.sample_R950.mechanical.impact: Charpy V-notch, room temperature, direction perpendicular to RD

truth.characterization_methods.sample_R650.pur: Local mechanical property mapping of phases
truth.characterization_methods.sample_R650.key: Carbide/ferrite lamellae hardness 3.6 GPa, δ-ferrite hardness 2.9 GPa.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_R850.key: Martensite 6.0 GPa, DIF 3.0 GPa, δ-ferrite 2.6 GPa.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_R950.key: Martensite 6.5 GPa, DIF 2.8 GPa, δ-ferrite 2.8 GPa.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_R650.pur: Phase-specific hardness measurement aligned with EBSD statistics
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_R650.key: C/F composite lamella hardness 3.6±0.6 GPa; δ-ferrite lamella hardness 2.9±0.3 GPa; hardness difference 0.72 GPa.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_R750.pur: Phase-specific hardness measurement aligned with EBSD statistics
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_R750.key: C/F composite lamella hardness 3.6±0.5 GPa; δ-ferrite lamella hardness 2.9±0.2 GPa.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_R850.pur: Phase-specific hardness measurement aligned with EBSD statistics
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_R850.key: Martensite hardness 6.0±0.5 GPa; δ-ferrite hardness 2.6±0.3 GPa; DIF hardness 3.0±0.3 GPa; maximum hardness difference 3.86 GPa.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_R950.pur: Phase-specific hardness measurement aligned with EBSD statistics
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_R950.key: Martensite hardness 6.5±0.5 GPa; δ-ferrite hardness 2.8±0.2 GPa; DIF hardness 2.8±0.4 GPa; maximum hardness difference 3.86 GPa.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Predict phase stability and carbide dissolution temperature
Calculated equilibrium phases: BCC, FCC, carbides; indicated carbide stability below 750 °C and austenite stability at high temperatures.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Predicted carbide stability below 750°C and δ+γ two-phase region at high temperatures, consistent with observed microstructures (complete carbide dissolution at ≥850°C, presence of austenite at 850–950°C).
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Explain dislocation pinning and strengthening by ultra-fine spherical carbides in ferrite matrix
Carbides with diameter <250 nm pin dislocations, increasing work hardening rate and crack propagation resistance in composite lamellae; effective for crack tip blunting when diameter <250 nm.
Explain interface bonding and crack propagation resistance at carbide/ferrite interfaces

Bonding force at carbide/ferrite interface exceeds ferrite/ferrite interface; nanoscale spherical carbides increase crack tip curvature radius reducing stress concentration factor inversely proportional to radius.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: SPP (strength-plasticity product) for R650: 16.5 ± 1.5 GPa%, R750: 12.5 ± 2.6 GPa%, R850: 9.6 ± 0.1 GPa%, R950: 4.4 ± 1.0 GPa%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: R650 impact crack initiation energy Ei = 59.6 J, propagation energy Ep = 147.9 J; R750 Ep = 51.51 J, indicating dominant role of propagation resistance.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: R850 and R950 impact curves show steep single peak with almost no Ei or Ep, indicating unstable crack propagation immediately after initiation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Low-temperature rolling zone (R650, R750) exhibits DRV (dynamic recovery) as dominant softening mechanism with high KAM values; high-temperature rolling zone (R850, R950) dominated by DRX (dynamic recrystallization) and DIFT, leading to HAGBs increase and dislocation density decrease.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Carbide precipitation mechanism: during low-temperature rolling, martensite decomposes, C, Mn, Cr diffuse to defect sites and precipitate as M3C, M23C6, M7C7; Ostwald ripening at higher temperature coarsens carbides.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: R650 fracture mode: mixed ductile fracture with crack blunting, deflection, and microvoid-induced horizontal propagation; tensile fracture shows delamination serrated morphology.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Orowan mechanism: carbides with diameter >10 nm pin dislocations, enhancing work hardening; nanoscale spherical carbides increase crack tip curvature radius and blunt crack, reducing stress concentration.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Bonding force at carbide/ferrite interface is greater than at ferrite/ferrite interface, preventing crack propagation through carbide-containing lamellae.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: In high-temperature rolling zone, hardness mismatch martensite/δ-ferrite >3.3 GPa causes interfacial stress concentration; DIF grains act as pre-fabricated crack paths.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Figure 4: EBSD maps (GB+GOS, KAM, IPF, nanoindentation) for all samples; Figure 5: mechanical properties including impact energy vs temperature, load-displacement curves, engineering stress-strain curves, work hardening rate curves; Figure 6: phase diagrams; Figure 7: TEM/EDS of R650 carbides; Figure 8: impact and tensile fracture morphology of R650; Figure 9: TEM/HRTEM of R650 and fracture schematics for all samples. Pure text pipeline does not include image pixel data.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: The tested steel was fabricated via VIM (vacuum induction melting); ingot homogenized at 1200 °C for 4 h, hot forged to 40 mm, then cut into specimens.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: Tensile specimen gauge length 25 mm, diameter 5 mm; crosshead speed 1.5 mm/min; Charpy specimen 10×10×55 mm, V-notch, impact direction perpendicular to RD.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: As-forged control microstructure (before rolling) showed no obvious lamellar structure, consisting mainly of coarse δ-ferrite and M/A constituents (Fig. 3a-c).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: R650 Charpy impact test detailed energy partition: crack initiation energy Ei = 59.6 J, propagation energy Ep = 147.9 J; R750 Ep = 51.51 J. High-temperature rolled specimens (R850, R950) exhibited steep single-peak impact load-displacement curves with immediate unstable crack propagation after initiation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Strength-plasticity product (SPP) values: R650 16.51 GPa%, R750 12.5 GPa%, R850 9.6 GPa%, R950 4.4 GPa%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Work hardening rate (θ) curves (Fig. 5e-f) for low-temperature rolled specimens showed a typical hump shape (rapid decrease at ε<0.02, slight rise, slow decay at ε≥0.03) due to Orowan pinning, whereas high-temperature rolled specimens showed monotonic decrease indicating lack of coordinated deformation capacity; exact numerical coordinates not provided in text.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Ostwald ripening mechanism caused carbide coarsening in R750: small carbides dissolve and diffuse via grain boundaries and dislocations to precipitate on larger carbides, producing coarse carbides at grain boundaries plus fine carbides inside grains.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: In the high-temperature rolling zone, DIFT produced equiaxed ferrite grains inside honeycomb martensite lamellae; DIF grains had higher C and Mn but lower Al than original δ-ferrite, contributing to higher hardness via solid solution and Hall-Petch strengthening.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Dislocation density was calculated using the formula ρ = 2θ/(μb) where θ is average KAM, μ is step size, and b is Burgers vector.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Phase evolution with temperature: at 650–750°C carbides are stable and form C/F composite lamellae; at 850–950°C carbides fully dissolve into austenite, resulting in δ-ferrite plus honeycomb martensite lamellae after cooling.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Low-temperature rolled specimens exhibited excellent deformation coordination between hard C/F composite lamellae and soft δ-ferrite lamellae, producing serrated tensile fracture morphology with delamination characteristics (Fig. 8c).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Tensile specimens were rod-shaped with gauge length 25 mm and diameter 5 mm sampled along rolling direction; Charpy V-notch specimens were 10 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm with notch on rolled surface and impact direction perpendicular to RD; tensile crosshead speed 1.5 mm/min.
